title: Leisure and Places of Interest
overview: The UK offers a wide range of leisure activities and places of interest. From famous landmarks and national parks to films, television and traditional pub culture, this lesson covers the key facts about how people spend their free time and the important places to know for the Life in the UK test.

study_sections.heading: British Cinema
study_sections.content:
The UK has had a major influence on modern cinema. Films were first shown publicly in the UK in 1896. From the beginning, British film makers became famous for clever special effects. British actors have worked in both the UK and the USA from the early days of cinema.

Sir Charles (Charlie) Chaplin became famous in silent movies for his tramp character and was one of many British actors to make a career in Hollywood.

British studios flourished in the 1930s. Eminent directors included Sir Alexander Korda and Sir Alfred Hitchcock, who later went to Hollywood and remained an important director until his death in 1980.

The 1950s and 1960s were a high point for British comedies, including Passport to Pimlico, The Ladykillers and the Carry On films.

Some of the most commercially successful films of all time, including the two highest-grossing film franchises (Harry Potter and James Bond), have been produced in the UK.

Ealing Studios has a claim to being the oldest continuously working film studio facility in the world.

Recent British actors to have won Oscars include Colin Firth, Sir Anthony Hopkins, Dame Judi Dench, Kate Winslet and Tilda Swinton.

The annual British Academy Film Awards, hosted by BAFTA, are the British equivalent of the Oscars.

Nick Park has won four Oscars for his animated films, including three for films featuring Wallace and Gromit.

study_sections.heading: Television and Radio
study_sections.content:
Everyone in the UK with a TV, computer or other medium which can be used for watching TV must have a television licence. One licence covers all equipment in one home, except where people rent different rooms in a shared house each with a separate tenancy agreement. People over 75 can apply for a free TV licence and blind people can get a 50% discount. The fine for watching TV without a licence is up to £1,000.

The money from TV licences is used to pay for the BBC (British Broadcasting Corporation). The BBC is the largest broadcaster in the world. It is the only wholly state-funded media organisation that is independent of government. Other UK channels are primarily funded through advertisements and subscriptions.

Popular programmes include soap operas such as Coronation Street and EastEnders.

The BBC started radio broadcasts in 1922 and began the world's first regular television service in 1936.

study_sections.heading: Leisure Activities
study_sections.content:
A lot of people have gardens at home and will spend their free time looking after them. Some people rent additional land called an allotment, where they grow fruit and vegetables.

Traditional foods associated with different parts of the UK include:
• England: Roast beef with potatoes, vegetables and Yorkshire puddings; fish and chips
• Wales: Welsh cakes - made from flour, dried fruits and spices
• Scotland: Haggis - a sheep's stomach stuffed with offal, suet, onions and oatmeal
• Northern Ireland: Ulster fry - bacon, eggs, sausage, black pudding, white pudding, tomatoes, mushrooms, soda bread and potato bread

Public houses (pubs) are an important part of UK social culture. To buy alcohol you must be 18 or over. When 16, people can drink wine or beer with a meal in a hotel or restaurant if with someone over 18. Pubs are usually open from 11.00 am (12 noon on Sundays).

To enter betting shops or gambling clubs you must be 18. The National Lottery has weekly draws - you must be 18 to participate. You can enter by buying a ticket or a scratch card.

All dogs in public places must wear a collar showing the owner's name and address. The owner is responsible for keeping the dog under control and cleaning up after it.

study_sections.heading: Places of Interest
study_sections.content:
There are 15 national parks in England, Wales and Scotland - areas of protected countryside that everyone can visit.

The National Trust (England, Wales and Northern Ireland) and the National Trust for Scotland are charities that preserve important buildings, coastline and countryside. The National Trust was founded in 1895 by three volunteers. There are now more than 61,000 volunteers.

Key UK landmarks:

Big Ben is the nickname for the great bell of the clock at the Houses of Parliament in London. The clock tower is officially named 'Elizabeth Tower' in honour of Queen Elizabeth II's Diamond Jubilee in 2012.

The Eden Project is in Cornwall. Its giant greenhouses house plants from all over the world. It is also a charity which runs environmental and social projects.

Edinburgh Castle has a long history dating back to the early Middle Ages. It is looked after by Historic Scotland.

The Giant's Causeway is on the north-east coast of Northern Ireland. It is a land formation of columns made from volcanic lava, formed about 50 million years ago.

Loch Lomond is the largest expanse of fresh water in mainland Britain. It is in the Loch Lomond and Trossachs National Park in west Scotland, which covers 720 square miles.

The London Eye is on the southern bank of the Thames - a Ferris wheel 443 feet (135 metres) tall. Originally built for the millennium celebrations.

Snowdon is the highest mountain in Wales. It is in Snowdonia National Park in North Wales, which covers 838 square miles.

The Tower of London was first built by William the Conqueror after 1066. Tours are given by Yeoman Warders (Beefeaters). Visitors can see the Crown Jewels there.

The Lake District is England's largest national park (885 square miles). It is famous for its lakes and mountains. The biggest lake is Windermere. In 2007, Wastwater was voted Britain's favourite view.

memory_hook: Remember landmarks by country: England (Tower of London, Eden Project, Lake District, Big Ben/Elizabeth Tower), Scotland (Edinburgh Castle, Loch Lomond, Giant's Causeway is Northern Ireland not Scotland!), Wales (Snowdon/Snowdonia), Northern Ireland (Giant's Causeway). TV licence fine = up to £1,000.

questions.prompt: When were films first shown publicly in the UK?
questions.explanation: Films were first shown publicly in the UK in 1896.

questions.prompt: What was Charlie Chaplin famous for?
options: His tramp character in silent movies
questions.explanation: Sir Charles Chaplin became famous in silent movies for his tramp character.

questions.prompt: Which are the two highest-grossing film franchises produced in the UK?
options: Lord of the Rings and Harry Potter
questions.explanation: The two highest-grossing film franchises produced in the UK are Harry Potter and James Bond.

questions.prompt: What is BAFTA?
options: British Academy of Film and Television Arts - hosts the British equivalent of the Oscars
questions.explanation: The annual British Academy Film Awards, hosted by BAFTA, are the British equivalent of the Oscars.

questions.prompt: Who needs a TV licence?
options: Everyone with a device for watching TV
Only those with a traditional TV set
questions.explanation: Everyone in the UK with a TV, computer or other medium for watching TV must have a television licence.

questions.prompt: What discount do blind people get on a TV licence?
questions.explanation: Blind people can get a 50% discount on a TV licence.

questions.prompt: What is the fine for watching TV without a licence?
questions.explanation: You will receive a fine of up to £1,000 if you watch TV but do not have a TV licence.

questions.prompt: What makes the BBC unique?
options: It is the only wholly state-funded media organisation independent of government
It is owned by the government
questions.explanation: The BBC is the only wholly state-funded media organisation that is independent of government.

questions.prompt: What age must you be to buy alcohol in a pub?
questions.explanation: To buy alcohol in a pub or night club you must be 18 or over.

questions.prompt: When can a 16-year-old drink wine or beer?
options: With a meal in a hotel or restaurant if with someone over 18
questions.explanation: When they are 16, people can drink wine or beer with a meal in a hotel or restaurant if with someone over 18.

questions.prompt: When do pubs open on Sundays?
questions.explanation: Pubs are usually open from 11.00 am, but from 12 noon on Sundays.

questions.prompt: What age must you be to play the National Lottery?
questions.explanation: People under 18 are not allowed to participate in the National Lottery.

questions.prompt: When was the National Trust founded?
questions.explanation: The National Trust was founded in 1895 by three volunteers.

questions.prompt: How many volunteers does the National Trust have?
questions.explanation: There are now more than 61,000 volunteers helping to keep the National Trust running.

questions.prompt: What is the official name of the Big Ben clock tower?
questions.explanation: The clock tower is named 'Elizabeth Tower' in honour of Queen Elizabeth II's Diamond Jubilee in 2012.

questions.prompt: Where is the Eden Project?
questions.explanation: The Eden Project is located in Cornwall, in the south west of England.

questions.prompt: Where is the Giant's Causeway?
questions.explanation: The Giant's Causeway is on the north-east coast of Northern Ireland.

questions.prompt: What is the Giant's Causeway made of?
options: Columns of volcanic lava, formed about 50 million years ago
questions.explanation: The Giant's Causeway is a land formation of columns made from volcanic lava, formed about 50 million years ago.

questions.prompt: What is Loch Lomond?
options: The largest expanse of fresh water in mainland Britain
questions.explanation: Loch Lomond is the largest expanse of fresh water in mainland Britain.

questions.prompt: How tall is the London Eye?
questions.explanation: The London Eye is a Ferris wheel that is 443 feet (135 metres) tall.

questions.prompt: What is the highest mountain in Wales?
questions.explanation: Snowdon is the highest mountain in Wales and is in Snowdonia National Park.

questions.prompt: Who first built the Tower of London?
questions.explanation: The Tower of London was first built by William the Conqueror after he became king in 1066.

questions.prompt: What are Yeoman Warders also known as?
questions.explanation: Tours of the Tower of London are given by Yeoman Warders, also known as Beefeaters.

questions.prompt: What is England's largest national park?
questions.explanation: The Lake District is England's largest national park, covering 885 square miles.

questions.prompt: Which lake is the biggest in the Lake District?
questions.explanation: The biggest stretch of water in the Lake District is Windermere.

questions.prompt: How many national parks are in England, Wales and Scotland?
questions.explanation: There are 15 national parks in England, Wales and Scotland.
